dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 responding for unconscious/fainting/near-fainting at Apt. in Richmond.
Hi, I'm Sarah, an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today, ma'am?
I don't feel well, like I might be going to vomit. Feels really strange.
Are you alert and oriented to person, place, and time, ma'am?
Yes, I'm Lucille. I live on Main Street, Virginia. Today is... Saturday, I believe.
Could you state the time for us, please?
It's almost 8 PM.
We'll need to take a look at your airway in a moment. Is there anything we should know beforehand?
No, I can breathe fine. Just feel really out of it.
I need to check your breathing and overall condition for assessment.
Your breathing is fine, 17 respirations per minute.
Oxygen saturation is at 95%, stable.
EKG is sinus rhythm with no immediate issues.
Your pulse is slow and regular at 79 per minute.
Blood pressure is 125 over 82. That's within normal range.
Blood glucose is 153. It's a bit on the high end, but not alarming.

dialogue.micro_intent: S.A.M.P.L.E. - events leading up to the event
dialogue.utterance: You mentioned feeling strange after eating. What did you do right before you began to feel ill, ma'am?
Went to dialysis this afternoon, then went to McDonalds. Got a Big Mac and fries. Soon after, started feeling funny.
May I ask about your current or recent health conditions, like chronic illness or surgeries?
I have an old heart attack, diabetic without insulin, kidney disease, and a recent kidney surgery.
On a scale of 0 to 10, what would you rate your current pain at?
I don't feel pain right now. Just nausea. I'd say a 0.
Your airway is patent with no secretions - everything looks clear.
Might I check your skin condition for any rashes or signs of injury?
Go ahead. It's a bit pale.
May I check your pupils? I'll be gentle with the flashlight.
Pupils are 3mm, equal, and reactive.
Can you take a deep breath for me while I listen to your lungs?
Lungs are clear and equal bilaterally. Chest rise and fall are equal.
I see a dialysis port on your right chest and a scar under your left breast. Any pain there?
No, they don't hurt.
We'll perform an EKG and start an IV for the possibility of supportive care. May we proceed?
Sure.
We've placed a 20G IV in your right arm so we can monitor and administer fluids if needed.
We're going to give you a shot of Zofran for the nausea since that's part of our protocol. Can we proceed?
I don't mind.
Administered 4 mg IV Ondansetron. Your condition appears to have improved.
Pulse is present, strong, and regular in all extremities.
We've selected protocol 3-9 for medical - nausea/vomiting.
Let's retake vitals to check our interventions, starting with blood pressure.
Pulse rate is still slow at 79 but steady. BP has dropped to 107 over 72.
Breathing is unlabored with 16 respirations per minute.
SpO2 has decreased to 92%, let me verify the reading.
EKG still shows a sinus rhythm with no alarming changes.
Glucose level is temporarily zero; it may have been an equipment error.
You are conscious, alert, and oriented to person, place, and time - you're CA&O x4.
We'll need to place a safety harness for transport. We'll make it as smooth as possible.
We'll take you to VCU ED, as requested in the initial call and based on your health history. Is that okay with you?
That's fine with me.
The stretcher will now be carried carefully from the house to the ambulance, one step at a time.
Please hold on to the straps we provide during transport. We'll make sure you stay secure the entire time.
Your two jackets will be brought in with you and remain with you during the transfer and ER stay.
We obtained a 12-lead EKG to be thorough, and it's been completed.
You appear generally sick. We'll make sure the hospital is aware of this.
Upon arrival at VCU, you'll be placed immediately into a room and meet directly with the staff.
Blood pressure is 122 over 76 on later assessment, and SpO2 is 93%.
We provided full care and assessment, and all documentation has been transferred directly to the hospital per standard protocol.
You are cleared for a smooth transfer and patient status has been explained in detail to the staff on duty.
We will monitor you continuously for any change in condition during the ride to the hospital.
The seat belts and safety harness will ensure a secure and comfortable ride during transport.
Your personal belongings and medications will remain with you the entire time.
Our overall goal is to ensure a safe and timely transport with the least discomfort or stress to you.
We'll do one final check of your vitals before leaving the scene.
Your pulse still registers at 79, and your blood pressure is stable at 122 over 76.
SpO2 has improved to 93%, and EKG remains in sinus rhythm.
VCU ED is a certified facility to manage your potential kidney disease and nausea complaints.
We're ready for transport, and the ambulance is prepared for a timely delivery to the hospital.
We will also be transferring a complete set of your vitals and EKG findings to the receiving RN.
We'll speak briefly with the receiving staff to ensure they're fully aware of your current condition.
Your medical history, including diabetes and dialysis schedule, has been detailed in the report.
You'll be placed in ED-22 with bed rails up to ensure your safety for any treatment or transfer.
Our staff will stay with you until the hospital takes over care and confirms handoff.

dialogue.role: nose, mouth and throat exam; Partner
dialogue.utterance: No drainage, hemorrhage, or obstruction of the airway or ears noted.

dialogue.role: nose, mouth and throat exam; Partner
dialogue.utterance: No apparent difficulty swallowing or signs of infection noted in the mouth or throat.
Heart sounds were assessed and found to be normal without murmur or irregular rhythm.
Please remain still during final loading into the ambulance. We'll be ready when you are.
All equipment is onboard and secure; we are prepared for a smooth transport process.
Per protocol 3-9, we've handled all appropriate interventions for medical- nausea/vomiting.
Your care has been consistent with the standards of our service and will continue in the hospital.
We've ensured your IV, vitals, and medication history are made known for continuity of care.
The hospital has received your report, and your equipment and belongings have been transferred.
Finalized vitals before departure: 122/76, 79, 17, SpO2 93%.
Hospital staff has accepted the patient, and the transition is complete.
